EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm brought widespread strong winds to northeast and eastern California on November 20. The storm also produced a modest snowfall from around Lake Tahoe northward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter two miles west of Loyalton reported winds sustained at 55 mph with gusts to 65 mph at 1133PST.  Additional high wind gusts include: 67 mph at Bull Flat RAWS at 1059PST and 69 mph at Doyle RAWS at 1211PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm brought widespread strong winds to northeast and eastern California on November 20. The storm also produced a modest snowfall from around Lake Tahoe northward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained between 40-50 mph on Lake Tahoe.  Truckee AWOS recorded a gust to 64 mph at 1255PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a cold front, temperatures across the region plummeted. The high at Fresno on November 12th was only 54 degrees, 10 degrees cooler than the previous day���s high and only 2 degrees above the record low maximum for the date, set in 1916. The morning of November 14th saw lows at the Wasco ALERT station and N.A.S. Lemoore of 29 degrees. A dry cold front moved through central California during the afternoon of the 14th, bringing another push of cold air into the region. Winds behind the front caused the development of upslope clouds along the foothills of the Southern Sierra Nevada. The next morning saw the first widespread hard freeze hit the Kern County deserts (the Western Mojave Desert), with lows in the coldest locations falling into the middle 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Most of the significant snowfall occurred above 6000 feet where 8 to 12 inches of snow was reported. In addition, gusty south winds were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions were reported. At Mountain High, 8 to 12 inches of snowfall was reported while Wrightwood reported 8 inches of snowfall. In addition to the snowfall, south winds gusting to 50 MPH were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions were reported. At Mount Pinos, 10 inches of snowfall was reported. In addition to the snowfall, south winds gusting to 50 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rain, flash flooding, gusty winds and severe thunderstorms to Central and Southern California. Rainfall amounts for this storm ranged from 1 to 3 inches over coastal areas to between 3 and 6 inches in the mountain and foothill areas. With such heavy rainfall, there were several reports of flash flooding along with mud and debris flows near the Station Fire burn area. In San Luis Obispo county, a severe thunderstorm producing very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the Station Fire burn area, intense showers and thunderstorms producing flash flooding as well as mud and debris flows along the Angeles Crest Highway. Near mile marker 23, over 90 vehicles were stuck in mud and debris. First hand reports from other areas around the Station Fire reported water flowing in canyons like raging rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly wind event buffeted sections of Los Angeles and Ventura counties with strong winds. The strongest winds were reported in the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties with wind gusts to 78 MPH recorded. However the strong and gusty winds did filter down into the Santa Clarita and San Fernando Valleys as well as the Antelope Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter in Palmdale reported sustained northwest winds of 45 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty winds to eastern California followed by a period of lake effect snow which brought several inches of snowfall to the south and southeast of Lake Tahoe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter just southwest of South Lake Tahoe reported 5 inches of snowfall. Four to five inches of snowfall was common to the south and east of Lake Tahoe (in the lake effect snow band), with up to 2 inches elsewhere around Lake Tahoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month of December, most areas had between 150% and 250% of normal precipitation, except in the high deserts where they had about 50% of normal. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor improved much of the region to D1 (moderate drought), while southwest San Bernardino and portions of central Riverside and extreme northeast Orange counties remained in a D2 (severe drought) classification.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, persistent 594 dm upper-level ridge over the West coast, combined with a 1045 mb surface high in the Great Basin and 30 to 40 kt 850 mb winds over Southern California led to strong Santa Ana winds on January 9th through the 13th. Wind gusts of up to 83 MPH were observed. The winds downed trees and power lines, overturned semi-trucks, and damaged roofs. ||Temperatures were well above normal during this period, with several days of record high maximum and minimum temperatures. On January 13th, the Santa Ana Fire Station recorded a minimum temperature of 73 degrees, the highest minimum temperature ever recorded for that station in the month of January, as well as the entire Winter season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 60 MPH was measured at Palm Avenue Elementary school in San Bernardino at 1339 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge of high pressure just off the West Coast and high surface pressure over the Great Basin brought locally strong, gusty offshore winds to the mountains and valleys of Southern California. The winds did cause some local damage to roofs, trees, power lines, freeway signs, and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 MPH wind gust was measured by the Fremont Canyon RAWS at 0330 PST.

The winds did cause some local damage to roofs, trees, power lines, freeway signs, and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 MPH wind gust was measured at Palm Avenue Elementary School in San Bernardino at 0752 PST. Shortly before, at approximately 0730 PST, a semi-tractor trailer overturned on Northbound Interstate 15 near Sierra Avenue in Fontana. Other damage in the area included a large metal freeway sign that was blown down onto Westbound Highway 210 near State Street in San Bernardino around 0900 PST. Around 1250 PST there were reports of power lines down in Ontario.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brush fire was started along Highway 76 near Verdugo Canyon Road when a tractor crashed into a power pole around 1130 PST on November 16th. The fire burned about 145 acres before being contained on the evening of the 17th. Four firefighters sustained injuries fighting the blaze, and no damage was reported.

A large, long period west swell reached the beaches of Southern California early on the 28th. Waves of 7 to 9 feet with some sets as high as 12 feet were reported. The swell and surf quickly subsided overnight. Two boats were capsized in the high surf, trapping 6 people. Only minor injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 25 foot fishing boat capsized around 0800 PST when returning to the San Diego Bay. Six people were on board and became trapped in the cabin. They managed to open the door and escape from the boat and were promptly rescued by the lifeguards. All six were admitted to the hospital, five of which were treated and released. The sixth person was kept and monitored overnight. A lifeguard boat flipped two hours later when trying to upright the overturned fishing boat. The two lifeguards on board were not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Offshore winds quickly developed on the evening of the 23rd.The strong gusty Northeast winds mainly impacted the Inland Empire below the Cajon Pass and the Santa Ana Mountains. There was one report of a car being blown off the road and one tree down. The winds diminished overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Fremont Canyon RAWS around 2130 PST and ended between 0130 PST and 0230 PST on the 24th. A peak wind gust of 74 MPH was measured between 2231 and 2330 PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were measured by the Julian CWOP between 0806 and 1107 PST. Between 0835 and 0848 PST multiple gusts of 65 MPH were measured.

The second powerful winter storm in a week raced through the region on December 12th and 13th. The storm brought high winds, heavy rain, and caused minor to moderate damage. Wind gusts in the high desert reached an impressive 100 MPH! The wind and heavy rain contributed to many downed trees, power outages, minor roadway flooding, and a flash flood. Parts of Highway 18 in the San Bernardino Mountains were closed due to large rocks on the roadway. In Perris, two people had to be rescued from a vehicle that had become trapped in a flooded intersection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 4 foot by 8 foot signs fell over on cars in Wrightwood due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second powerful winter storm in a week raced through the region on December 12th and 13th. The storm brought high winds, heavy rain, and caused minor to moderate damage. Wind gusts in the high desert reached an impressive 100 MPH! The wind and heavy rain contributed to many downed trees, power outages, minor roadway flooding, and a flash flood. Parts of Highway 18 in the San Bernardino Mountains were closed due to large rocks on the roadway. In Perris, two people had to be rescued from a vehicle that had become trapped in a flooded intersection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive runoff from the heavy rain in the San Bernardino Mountains produced strong currents in the Whitewater River wash, which caused an earthen berm at a percolation basin in Palm Springs to collapse. The water then continued to flow through the wash and across several streets, bringing large amounts of mud and debris with it. Indian Canyon Road, Gene Autry Road, and Vista Chino road, all of which lie across the wash's natural path, were closed due to flooding. Three cars were swept off the road during the flood - two on Indian Canyon Drive and one on Vista Chino. One of the percolation ponds also sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds developed over the Tehachapi Mountains on March 27th, and downsloped into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. Temperatures warmed to well above normal, with Bakersfield reaching a high of 85 degrees. The low at Meadows Field on the 27th was 56 degrees, which was just 1 degree from the record high minimum temperature for the date (57 degrees in 1978). The winds subsided during the night as an upper-level ridge of high pressure moved over the San Joaquin Valley. This ridge brought continued well-above-normal temperatures to the Valley on March 28th. Fresno had a high of 81 degrees, the first 80-degree day since November 18th, 2008. Bakersfield warmed to 87 degrees, just missing a 106-year-old record high of 88, set in 1893. The 87 degrees at Meadows Field was the warmest day in March for Bakersfield. Likewise, Fresno���s 81 degrees was its warmest day for the month.|An upper-level disturbance tracked across the Pacific Northwest during the night of March 28th-29th, then dropped into the Great Basin. Although this disturbance did not bring any precipitation to the region, it did bring gusty winds to the Kern County|mountains and deserts on the 29th, and even some locally gusty winds to the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening. Gusts to around 40 mph were widespread across the San Joaquin Valley floor, and gusts in the mountains and deserts were between 45 and 50 mph.|March ended with a dry upper-level disturbance moving into the Pacific Northwest and California. This disturbance brought another round of gusty west winds to the Kern County mountains and deserts during the evening of the 31st, with winds gusting between 40 and 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March began with an upper-level low off the California coast. Strong warming in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley gave Bakersfield a record high of 86 degrees, which almost was the warmest day of the month at Meadows Field. The low drew subtropical moisture into interior central California from the southwest, bringing rain and high-elevation snow to the region. In Yosemite National Park, a foot of new snow fell at Tuolumne Meadows during the night of March 2nd-3rd.|Colder air moved into the region during the 3rd as a slow moving cold front dropped south. The high at Fresno dropped from 67 on the 3rd to only 59 on the 4th, and Bakersfield���s high fell 13 degrees���from 74 on the 3rd to only 61 the next day. More snow fell over the Southern Sierra Nevada beginning the night of March 3rd and continuing through the 4th. Estimated snowfalls at SNOTEL sites ranged from 15 inches to around 2 feet. With a cold, unstable airmass over the San Joaquin Valley, conditions were right for thunderstorms to form over the west side of the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening of March 4th.|The trough pulled east of California during the morning of March 7th. This system brought the bulk of March���s precipitation to central California, with Fresno having a storm total of 0.18 inch, and Bakersfield receiving 0.20 inch. With the moist lower levels of the airmass over the San Joaquin Valley, patchy fog developed during the morning hours of the 7th.

An upper-level trough coupled with a 125 MPH jet max and strong onshore pressure gradients brought light rain and strong winds to Southern California on March 22nd. Gusty winds knocked down trees, freeway signs, and power lines in the mountains and deserts. Two fires were reported during this time, both of which caused damage to structures and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vegetation fire fueled by gusty winds spread to a single family home near La Quinta and partially destroyed the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough coupled with a 125 MPH jet max and strong onshore pressure gradients brought light rain and strong winds to Southern California on March 22nd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vegetation fire fueled by gusty winds spread through Lake Cahuilla Park near La Quinta. The fire destroyed playground equipment, an awning of a building, and 35 palm trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low moving over Northern California combined with strong onshore surface pressure gradients led to strong gusty winds in the mountains and deserts of Southern California. The high winds resulted in downed power lines, minor roof damage. A small fire, fueled by the strong winds, burned at least two homes in Palm Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts in excess of 58 MPH were measured at the Burns Canyon RAWS between 1651 and 1851 PST. A peak gust of 64 MPH was measured at 1651 PST.

The high winds resulted in downed power lines, minor roof damage. A small fire, fueled by the strong winds, burned at least two homes in Palm Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 245 PST, a trained spotter reported wind gusts up to 76 MPH in Lucerne Valley. The high winds caused some damage to a shed and blew down an antenna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low moving over Northern California combined with strong onshore surface pressure gradients led to strong gusty winds in the mountains and deserts of Southern California. The high winds resulted in downed power lines, minor roof damage. A small fire, fueled by the strong winds, burned at least two homes in Palm Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Whitewater RAWS at 550 PST and continued through 2050 PST. At 550 and 650 PST peak wind gusts of 69 MPH were measured. ||There were numerous reports of damage throughout the Coachella Valley. Several power lines and traffic signals were downed in the high winds, causing power outages to approximately 1,900 customers. Many trees suffered broken limbs, and about 40 trees were uprooted. Two tractor-trailers overturned on Interstate 10. Damage to structures included roofs being torn off buildings and damaged bleachers at the Palm Springs Stadium.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwest to north pressure gradients developed over Southern California, producing strong winds across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties as well as the Antelope Valley. Automated sensors in these areas reported wind gusts to 61 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Lake Palmdale reported a northwest wind gust of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 50 acre brush fire in Palm Springs, driven by strong gusty winds, damaged two homes and led to the evacuation of 50 other homes. The fire began in the late afternoon, burning desert scrub near a residential neighborhood. The fire was contained later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire began in sparse desert brush near a residential neighborhood. Strong, gusty winds spread the fire towards the neighborhood, resulting in the damage of two homes and the evacuation of approximately 50 other homes. No major injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds developed over the Tehachapi Mountains on March 27th, and downsloped into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. Temperatures warmed to well above normal, with Bakersfield reaching a high of 85 degrees. The low at Meadows Field on the 27th was 56 degrees, which was just 1 degree from the record high minimum temperature for the date (57 degrees in 1978). The winds subsided during the night as an upper-level ridge of high pressure moved over the San Joaquin Valley. This ridge brought continued well-above-normal temperatures to the Valley on March 28th. Fresno had a high of 81 degrees, the first 80-degree day since November 18th, 2008. Bakersfield warmed to 87 degrees, just missing a 106-year-old record high of 88, set in 1893. The 87 degrees at Meadows Field was the warmest day in March for Bakersfield. Likewise, Fresno���s 81 degrees was its warmest day for the month.|An upper-level disturbance tracked across the Pacific Northwest during the night of March 28th-29th, then dropped into the Great Basin. Although this disturbance did not bring any precipitation to the region, it did bring gusty winds to the Kern County|mountains and deserts on the 29th, and even some locally gusty winds to the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening. Gusts to around 40 mph were widespread across the San Joaquin Valley floor, and gusts in the mountains and deserts were between 45 and 50 mph.|March ended with a dry upper-level disturbance moving into the Pacific Northwest and California. This disturbance brought another round of gusty west winds to the Kern County mountains and deserts during the evening of the 31st, with winds gusting between 40 and 50 mph.

A dry system moved through California on March 8th and 9th, bringing only some high clouds and locally gusty winds to the Kern County mountains and deserts. Naval Air Station Lemoore had a low of only 24 degrees, while the airports at Merced and Porterville dropped to 27 degrees.

An upper-level trough coupled with a 125 MPH jet max and strong onshore pressure gradients brought light rain and strong winds to Southern California on March 22nd. Gusty winds knocked down trees, freeway signs, and power lines in the mountains and deserts. Two fires were reported during this time, both of which caused damage to structures and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in Ocotillo Wells between 1303 and 1324 PST. At 1317 PST, a peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured.||Along Interstate 8 near In-Ko-Pah, large freeway signs blew down onto the Eastbound lanes. High winds also triggered a rock slide along San Diego County Road S22 between mile markers 13.5 and 15.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm from the Northern Pacific Ocean brought strong winds to the Central Coast of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused an estimated 100 foot tall, five foot diameter based pine tree to fall onto power lines at the junction of Shoreline Highway and Panoramic Highway around 11:10 a.m. PDT. The intersection was closed for five hours as crews removed the debris. The seven customers who lost power had it restored by 6:30 p.m. PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms brought rain and mountain snow on February 5th through the 9th. Another series of cold fronts followed on the 12th through the 14th, bringing more rain and snow to the region. A large Pacific storm brought significant rain and mountain snow on the 16th and 17th. For the month, all sampling sites were near or above 90% of normal precipitation, with most  above 110% for the month. For the season to date, most stations were between 80 and 115% of normal. The Governor issued a State of Emergency for the entire State of California on February 27th due to the ongoing drought, calling for all Californians to immediately decrease their water use. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor continues the northwest portion of the region in the D1 classification (Moderate Drought), with the rest of the area lifted to the D0 classification (Abnormally Dry).

February began with an upper-level ridge of high pressure building into California, which promoted dense fog development around the valley during the morning of the 1st of the month. West of the ridge, a low-pressure trough deepened as it dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska. Once the ridge had moved inland on the 3rd and 4th, the combination of the ridge and trough set up a southerly flow over the state. These winds funneled through the passes of the Tehachapi Mountains into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, with gusts in the far south end of the Valley reaching 55 mph. The winds warmed as they descended through the passes, bringing unseasonably warm temperatures to the San Joaquin Valley. Bakersfield set record highs on both the 3rd and the 4th, with the high of February 4th reaching 85 degrees. Fresno tied its record high of 74 on the 4th.

High clouds streaming into interior central California on February 20th heralded the arrival of the next winter storm. This storm moved through the region on the 21st and 22nd, bringing more rain and mountain snow. Over a foot of snow fell on the Southern Sierra Nevada from Yosemite south to Kings Canyon, while 8-10 inches fell in the Tulare County mountains. Skies cleared over the San Joaquin Valley on February 23rd, except for some clouds along the east side of the Valley and over the south end. A northwest flow over the San Joaquin Valley during the night of the 24th-25th pushed these clouds into the Valley-facing slopes of the Tehachapi Mountains and the Kern County portion of the Sierra Nevada, including the passes. As the clouds pushed into the Tehachapi Pass and contacted the surface, some dense fog developed and persisted through the night into daybreak.

February began with an upper-level ridge of high pressure building into California, which promoted dense fog development around the valley during the morning of the 1st of the month. West of the ridge, a low-pressure trough deepened as it dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska. Once the ridge had moved inland on the 3rd and 4th, the combination of the ridge and trough set up a southerly flow over the state. These winds funneled through the passes of the Tehachapi Mountains into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, with gusts in the far south end of the Valley reaching 55 mph. The winds warmed as they descended through the passes, bringing unseasonably warm temperatures to the San Joaquin Valley. Bakersfield set record highs on both the 3rd and the 4th, with the high of February 4th reaching 85 degrees. Fresno tied its record high of 74 on the 4th.

Tule fog returned to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on December 14th, and increased in coverage each day through the 17th. The fog lifted into a stratus deck during the afternoon of the 17th and persisted through the afternoon of the 19th, as a ridge of high pressure became established over the area. The stratus started eroding from the southern San Joaquin during the late morning of the 19th, then kept clearing toward the central part of the valley by the afternoon. Some patchy fog was observed on the night on the 19th-20th, and again on the night of the 20th-21st. Rain returned to the valley with snow in the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada (started at around 9000 feet) on the 21st as another trough of low pressure that over the area. By the morning of the 22nd, the cold air behind the low pressure center moved over the area, causing gusty winds even over the San Joaquin Valley, and snow levels lowered dramatically (below 2500 feet by the late morning). Interstate 5 over the Grapevine was closed during the morning of the 22nd due to snow. Winds gusted to 40 mph at Fresno Yosemite International Airport on the 22nd and to 46 mph at the Madera Municipal Airport. Winds gusted as high as 78 mph in the Kern County deserts, and a few dead trees were toppled near Tehachapi by gusts in the 40-45 mph range.||The cold air remained trapped in the San Joaquin Valley after the storm moved east of the region. Freezing temperatures were reported on the morning of December 24th, with lows in the rural areas dropping into the mid 20s. Clouds ahead of the next storm moved into the region Christmas Day, limiting the extent of the freezing temperatures and heralding the arrival of more precipitation to the region.

Skies cleared over the San Joaquin Valley on February 23rd, except for some clouds along the east side of the Valley and over the south end. A northwest flow over the San Joaquin Valley during the night of the 24th-25th pushed these clouds into the Valley-facing slopes of the Tehachapi Mountains and the Kern County portion of the Sierra Nevada, including the passes. As the clouds pushed into the Tehachapi Pass and contacted the surface, some dense fog developed and persisted through the night into daybreak.

A series of storms brought rain and mountain snow on February 5th through the 9th. Another series of cold fronts followed on the 12th through the 14th, bringing more rain and snow to the region. A large Pacific storm brought significant rain and mountain snow on the 16th and 17th. For the month, all sampling sites were near or above 90% of normal precipitation, with most  above 110% for the month. For the season to date, most stations were between 80 and 115% of normal. The Governor issued a State of Emergency for the entire State of California on February 27th due to the ongoing drought, calling for all Californians to immediately decrease their water use. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor continues the northwest portion of the region in the D1 classification (Moderate Drought), with the rest of the area lifted to the D0 classification (Abnormally Dry).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell on top of a recently burned forest causing flash flooding and debris flows. More than three feet of mud covered State Route One closing it for several hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep marine layer brought light rain and drizzle to the coastal areas the first two days of the month, with accumulations less than one tenth of an inch. On the 3rd, anomalous low pressure off the central California coast triggered bands of convection. Rainfall reports were mostly less than one tenth inch, with locally heavier amounts. Another wave moved over the area on the 16th, bringing light rain to San Diego county. Accumulations were once again under one tenth of an inch. An isolated thunderstorm in Wrightwood produced a third of an inch of rain. Most areas in the mountain, valley, and coastal locations ended the season with 60 to 80 percent of normal precipitation, while the desert areas received 75 to 100 percent of normal.||The State of Emergency for the continued drought remained in effect. Water restrictions in some areas remained in effect, though no shortages have been reported. A level 2 drought alert remained in effect for the City of San Diego, which restricts water usage. Water cut-backs for some agricultural purposes went into effect, mainly impacting the citrus and avocado orchards in San Diego and southern Riverside counties. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor placed all areas except for the deserts in the D2 (Severe Drought) category, while the deserts remained in the D1 (Moderate Drought) category.
High temperatures over 110 degrees in Death Valley National Park contributed to the death of one man.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man went missing in Death Valley National Park on May 19th, and was found dead the next day.  The cause of death was heat related disorder, compounded by other health issues.

Through the Interstate 5 corridor in Ventura and Los Angeles counties, northerly wind gusts between 69 and 85 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Montecito reported north winds with sustained speeds between 40 and 49 MPH with gusts to 63 MPH.

A series of thunderstorms in the San Bernardino County deserts caused some structural damage in the Hesperia area. Lightning from the thunderstorm also began a small brush fire near Yucca Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a possible microburst or gustnado damaged four horse shelter roofs near the intersection of Cottonwood Avenue and Joshua Street in Hesperia. One 10 foot by 10 foot metal and wood roof was completely removed from the shelter. Thunderstorm winds also knocked over power lines in Hesperia and Victorville earlier in the day.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two vegetation fires began on May 27th near the Lake Perris State Recreation Area. The fire led to an evaction of the Lake Perris State Recreation Area campground and several roads were closed in the area.The 503 acre fire was contained around 1700 PST on May 28th. The cause of the fire was determined to be arson. No damage to structures or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate vegetation fires were started by an arsonist around 1447 PST on May 27th. The fires spread over the next twenty-four hours, burning a total of 503 acres. The fires caused the evacuation of parts of the Lake Perris State Recreation Area and the closure of several roads. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small fire fueled by gusty winds destroyed a shed and an abandoned house in Otay Mesa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four acre fire began around 1145 PST in Otay Mesa along Airway Road near Caliente Avenue. The fire spread through light grass and into a pile of tires. A shed and an abandoned house were destroyed by the blaze. The fire was contained by about 1245 PST. No injuries were reported.

Water restrictions in some areas remained in effect, though no shortages have been reported. A level 2 drought alert remained in effect for the City of San Diego, which restricts water usage. Water cut-backs for some agricultural purposes went into effect, mainly impacting the citrus and avocado orchards in San Diego and southern Riverside counties. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor placed all areas except for the deserts in the D2 (Severe Drought) category, while the deserts remained in the D1 (Moderate Drought) category.

The Jesusita Fire burned over 8,700 acres in the hills above the city of Santa Barbara. This wildfire was driven by a combination of a large dead fuel bed and Sundowner winds gusting over 60 MPH. The damage as a result of this fire was significant with 80 homes destroyed and another 15 homes badly damaged. No deaths were reported but at least 30 firefighters were injured battling the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jesusita Wildfire burned 8,733 acres of land in the hills above the city of Santa Barbara. The fire was driven by a combination of a large dead fuel bed and gusty Sundowner winds. Automated sensors in the vicinity of the fire reported northerly wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH for many hours which were supported by observations from the fireline. Overall, 80 homes were destroyed and another 15 homes were badly damaged. No deaths were reported, but at least 30 firefighters reported some type of injury. Suppression costs were in excess of $17 million with no report of the amount of property damage.

Monsoon moisture continued to fuel scattered thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  One storm produced high winds along the Colorado River, and another cluster of storms produced lightning damage in Las Vegas.
A push of monsoon moisture triggered scattered thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. A few storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding stranded several people in vehicles on Hart Mine Road in the Mojave National Preserve.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance off the west coast of Baja California spun a fetch of subtropical moisture into California beginning July 16th, with isolated thunderstorms developing along the Southern Sierra Nevada crest. By the 18th, there was enough moisture that thunderstorms also formed in the Kern County deserts, with one afternoon thunderstorm near Edwards AFB reaching severe criteria with gusts to 67 mph. Thunderstorms over the Sierra and the Kern desert redeveloped the next day, and spread into the Tehachapi Mountains. Around an inch of rain fell in the Frazier Park-Pine Mountain Club area, but no flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NASA wind sensor at the South Lake Bed near Edwards AFB reported severe wind gusts from the outflow of a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lion Wildfire, located in Sequoia National Forest, ignited on 6/30/09 at 1030 PDT due to lightning strikes. The location was 3 miles northwest of the Blackrock Ranger Station on the Kern Plateau in Tulare County. The size was 2023 acres. There were no fatalities or structures lost directly due to the wildfire. The cost to containment was  $528,190.

A series of storms brought rain and mountain snow on February 5th through the 9th. Another series of cold fronts followed on the 12th through the 14th, bringing more rain and snow to the region. A large Pacific storm brought significant rain and mountain snow on the 16th and 17th. For the month, all sampling sites were near or above 90% of normal precipitation, with most  above 110% for the month. For the season to date, most stations were between 80 and 115% of normal. The Governor issued a State of Emergency for the entire State of California on February 27th due to the ongoing drought, calling for all Californians to immediately decrease their water use. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor continues the northwest portion of the region in the D1 classification (Moderate Drought), with the rest of the area lifted to the D0 classification (Abnormally Dry).

February began with an upper-level ridge of high pressure building into California, which promoted dense fog development around the valley during the morning of the 1st of the month. West of the ridge, a low-pressure trough deepened as it dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska. Once the ridge had moved inland on the 3rd and 4th, the combination of the ridge and trough set up a southerly flow over the state. These winds funneled through the passes of the Tehachapi Mountains into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, with gusts in the far south end of the Valley reaching 55 mph. The winds warmed as they descended through the passes, bringing unseasonably warm temperatures to the San Joaquin Valley. Bakersfield set record highs on both the 3rd and the 4th, with the high of February 4th reaching 85 degrees. Fresno tied its record high of 74 on the 4th.

A weak trough of low pressure brought mid-level moisture over the area on the 18th and 19th, leading to scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms over the mountains and deserts. No measurable precipitation was reported. Another weak upper low and a deep marine layer brought scattered light rain from the 29th through 31st. Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over the northern mountain and desert areas on the 29th, resulting in less than one-quarter inch of rain. On the 30th, coastal San Diego county received widespread light rain, with amounts totaling less than one-tenth inch.||The State of Emergency for the State of California due to the ongoing drought continued. Water restrictions were in force in many areas, however, no water shortages have been reported. The San Diego County Water Authority continued the Level 2 drought alert. At the end of the month, the Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor had the entire area classified as D1 (moderate drought).

Tule fog returned to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on December 14th, and increased in coverage each day through the 17th. The fog lifted into a stratus deck during the afternoon of the 17th and persisted through the afternoon of the 19th, as a ridge of high pressure became established over the area. The stratus started eroding from the southern San Joaquin during the late morning of the 19th, then kept clearing toward the central part of the valley by the afternoon. Some patchy fog was observed on the night on the 19th-20th, and again on the night of the 20th-21st. Rain returned to the valley with snow in the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada (started at around 9000 feet) on the 21st as another trough of low pressure that over the area. By the morning of the 22nd, the cold air behind the low pressure center moved over the area, causing gusty winds even over the San Joaquin Valley, and snow levels lowered dramatically (below 2500 feet by the late morning). Interstate 5 over the Grapevine was closed during the morning of the 22nd due to snow. Winds gusted to 40 mph at Fresno Yosemite International Airport on the 22nd and to 46 mph at the Madera Municipal Airport. Winds gusted as high as 78 mph in the Kern County deserts, and a few dead trees were toppled near Tehachapi by gusts in the 40-45 mph range.||The cold air remained trapped in the San Joaquin Valley after the storm moved east of the region. Freezing temperatures were reported on the morning of December 24th, with lows in the rural areas dropping into the mid 20s. Clouds ahead of the next storm moved into the region Christmas Day, limiting the extent of the freezing temperatures and heralding the arrival of more precipitation to the region.

A deep marine layer brought light rain and drizzle to the coastal areas the first two days of the month, with accumulations less than one tenth of an inch. On the 3rd, anomalous low pressure off the central California coast triggered bands of convection. Rainfall reports were mostly less than one tenth inch, with locally heavier amounts. Another wave moved over the area on the 16th, bringing light rain to San Diego county. Accumulations were once again under one tenth of an inch. An isolated thunderstorm in Wrightwood produced a third of an inch of rain. Most areas in the mountain, valley, and coastal locations ended the season with 60 to 80 percent of normal precipitation, while the desert areas received 75 to 100 percent of normal.||The State of Emergency for the continued drought remained in effect. Water restrictions in some areas remained in effect, though no shortages have been reported. A level 2 drought alert remained in effect for the City of San Diego, which restricts water usage. Water cut-backs for some agricultural purposes went into effect, mainly impacting the citrus and avocado orchards in San Diego and southern Riverside counties. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor placed all areas except for the deserts in the D2 (Severe Drought) category, while the deserts remained in the D1 (Moderate Drought) category.

A strong low pressure trough approached the California coast during the night of December 9th-10th. High clouds ahead of the trough helped moderate overnight lows, preventing a third night of a widespread freeze. Then rain came to the San Joaquin Valley with snow in the mountains. The greatest snow accumulations were above the 7000 feet elevation on Dec 11th through the 13th, due to a series of low pressure systems associated with the general trough pattern. Snowfall amounts from the 12th through the morning of the 13th averaged around 3 feet above 7000 feet with locally higher amounts to nearly 5 feet. Temperatures warmed on the 11th as a subtropical feed that moved over the area, bringing plenty of moisture. The warm, moist airmass also triggered isolated thunderstorms over the central and southern San Joaquin Valley. There was heavy rain in the mountains up to around the 7000-foot elevation on the 11th and 12th, prior to changing to snow during the night of the 12th. The snow continued through the day on the 13th, with snow levels dropping to around 5000 feet as cooler air filtered into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The California Highway Patrol reported 2 vehicles were stuck in flood waters and the intersection of Southgate Drive and South M Street.

A strong low pressure trough approached the California coast during the night of December 9th-10th. High clouds ahead of the trough helped moderate overnight lows, preventing a third night of a widespread freeze. Then rain came to the San Joaquin Valley with snow in the mountains. The greatest snow accumulations were above the 7000 feet elevation on Dec 11th through the 13th, due to a series of low pressure systems associated with the general trough pattern. Snowfall amounts from the 12th through the morning of the 13th averaged around 3 feet above 7000 feet with locally higher amounts to nearly 5 feet. Temperatures warmed on the 11th as a subtropical feed that moved over the area, bringing plenty of moisture. The warm, moist airmass also triggered isolated thunderstorms over the central and southern San Joaquin Valley. There was heavy rain in the mountains up to around the 7000-foot elevation on the 11th and 12th, prior to changing to snow during the night of the 12th. The snow continued through the day on the 13th, with snow levels dropping to around 5000 feet as cooler air filtered into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported water up to 1 and a half feet deep at the intersection of CA-99 and Jenson Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tule fog returned to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on December 14th, and increased in coverage each day through the 17th. The fog lifted into a stratus deck during the afternoon of the 17th and persisted through the afternoon of the 19th, as a ridge of high pressure became established over the area. The stratus started eroding from the southern San Joaquin during the late morning of the 19th, then kept clearing toward the central part of the valley by the afternoon. Some patchy fog was observed on the night on the 19th-20th, and again on the night of the 20th-21st. Rain returned to the valley with snow in the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada (started at around 9000 feet) on the 21st as another trough of low pressure that over the area. By the morning of the 22nd, the cold air behind the low pressure center moved over the area, causing gusty winds even over the San Joaquin Valley, and snow levels lowered dramatically (below 2500 feet by the late morning). Interstate 5 over the Grapevine was closed during the morning of the 22nd due to snow. Winds gusted to 40 mph at Fresno Yosemite International Airport on the 22nd and to 46 mph at the Madera Municipal Airport. Winds gusted as high as 78 mph in the Kern County deserts, and a few dead trees were toppled near Tehachapi by gusts in the 40-45 mph range.||The cold air remained trapped in the San Joaquin Valley after the storm moved east of the region. Freezing temperatures were reported on the morning of December 24th, with lows in the rural areas dropping into the mid 20s. Clouds ahead of the next storm moved into the region Christmas Day, limiting the extent of the freezing temperatures and heralding the arrival of more precipitation to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven-tenths of an inch of rainfall occurred in just one hour near Dunlap in Fresno County.

A series of storms brought rain and mountain snow on February 5th through the 9th. Another series of cold fronts followed on the 12th through the 14th, bringing more rain and snow to the region. A large Pacific storm brought significant rain and mountain snow on the 16th and 17th. For the month, all sampling sites were near or above 90% of normal precipitation, with most  above 110% for the month. For the season to date, most stations were between 80 and 115% of normal. The Governor issued a State of Emergency for the entire State of California on February 27th due to the ongoing drought, calling for all Californians to immediately decrease their water use. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor continues the northwest portion of the region in the D1 classification (Moderate Drought), with the rest of the area lifted to the D0 classification (Abnormally Dry).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Northern and Central California producing strong wind and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind sent numerous tree limbs into power lines resulting in power outages in Santa Cruz County. The strong wind combined with saturated soils from heavy rain to uproot trees causing road closures and additional power outages as trees knocked power lines to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind ahead of a cold front brought down trees and produced brief heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees fell in western Sonoma County during this storm. One of those trees killed a 49-year-old woman as she walked along Freestone Flat Road near Freestone. The oak tree was approximately 60 feet tall.

An upper-level ridge built into California beginning May 27th, bringing another warming trend to interior central California. Fresno warmed to 99 degrees on the 27th, and reached 102 the next day. A weak low off Baja California spun mid-level moisture into California from the south, and the combination of heat and moisture triggered strong thunderstorms over the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains each afternoon and evening, beginning on May 27th and continuing through the end of the month. Hail up to 1.5 inch in diameter fell near the town of Orange Cove on the evening of the 28th, and as thunderstorms over the Sierra Nevada, and over San Luis Obispo County, collapsed, outflow winds pushed across the San Joaquin Valley, triggering thunderstorms over the eastern and central Valley. Two of the storms merged into a strong system that produced gusty winds that toppled numerous trees on the Valley floor, as well as 1-inch hail near Visalia. Strong winds also triggered a gust-front tornado (gustnado) that damaged a mobile home park in Orosi.||Convection was weaker on May 29th, as thunderstorms developed earlier and several of the storms were pushed north of the region by a more southeasterly flow aloft. However, this southeast flow also carried thunderstorms from northeastern Los Angeles and northwestern San Bernardino Counties into southeastern Kern County near Rosamond, Edwards AFB, and Boron. One thunderstorm collapsed as it passed west of Edwards AFB in the early afternoon, generating outflow winds that were measured at a peak of 62 knots (71 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee reported trees down and near zero visibility in blowing dust along SR 198 just east of Visalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure center off the coast of Northern California produced a few light showers over the area on August 3rd and 4th. During the third week of the month, monsoonal flow produced scattered showers and thunderstorms. The highest rainfall amounts for the month, mainly due to isolated thunderstorms, were under one-quarter inch. The State of Emergency for California remained in place, as well as previously enforced water restrictions for agricultural and residential areas. No water shortages have been reported. By the end of August, the entire area was classified as D2 (Severe Drought) by the Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Oak Glen III Fire started on August 30th around 1:46 PM PDT near the community of Oak Glen in the San Bernardino National Forest. Mandatory evacuations were ordered for parts of Oak Glen. The fire burned 1,159 acres before being contained on September 8th. Approximately 2,000 homes were threatened. One residence and one outbuilding were destroyed, and one other residence was damaged. Four injuries were reported, though the details are unknown. The fire cost approximately 6.9 million dollars to fight.

The Pendleton Fire began at approximately 3:30 PM PDT on August 31st near Yucaipa. Evacuations were ordered for 1,100 homes and some roads were closed. One residential structure and one outbuilding were destroyed in the 860 acre fire, which was contained on September 3rd. One injury was reported. The cause of the 1.49 million dollar fire is still under investigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Harden wildfire located in Yosemite NP, ignited on June 7th at 2301 PST due to lightning. The location was 13 miles north of Yosemite Valley in Mariposa County. The size was 1661 acres. Containment was on July 10th. Cost to containment was $880,000.

Strong south winds occurred again ahead of a strong cold front across Modoc county. South to southwest sustained winds of 38-45 mph were observed on November 20th with a peak gust of 67 mph across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alturas, California ASOS measured sustained south winds of 38-45 mph with gusts of 62-64 mph between 20/0955 and 20/1055 PST. The peak gust of 64 mph was observed at 20/1055 PST. Juniper Creek RAWS measured a peak gust of 67 mph at 20/1052 PST while Modoc NWR RAWS measured a peak gust of 64 mph at 20/1132 PST.|The Alturas Record newspaper reported winds 40-50 mph with gusts up to 72 mph.  Damage was reported as early as 20/0900 PST when tin roofing was reported airborne near Carlos St.  Several trees reported down along with broken power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought six to ten inches of snow to the area.

CZ_NAME: EAST BAY HILLS AND THE DIABLO RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moved south along the west coast of California causing damaging winds in portions of the state's northern and central coast on Saturday. Over 180,000 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measures peak wind gusts observed at RAWS:  Mallory Ridge, elevation 1760 feet - 63 mph at 8:45 a.m.; Diablo, elevation 3849 feet - 72 mph at 10:17 p.m.; and Poverty, elevation 2066 feet - 61 mph at 12:18 a.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frigid Arctic air mass moved into Oregon December 5th and remained over the area through December 12th, when it finally abated as a Pacific system approached the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures in this zone during this interval were generally in the single digits above zero.  On the coldest nights...the 8th through the 10th, the coldest stations dipped below zero.  The coldest stations had lows ranging from 6 to 12 degrees below zero.  Temperatures warmed on the 11th but remained well below normal...and became more seasonable on the 12th.  Numerous broken pipes and other cold-related damage was reported during this interval.

Winds gusted to 50 to 60 mph over higher elevations which resulted in poor visibilities. Highway travel on Interstate 80, US 50, and the other trans-Sierra pass roads was impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system bought showers and thunderstorms across the Central Valley. Lightning and small hail were reported with these storms, along with a gustnado spotted near Wheatland. A woman was struck by lightning in her home near Marysville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gustnado was sighted just southwest of Wheatland ahead of an approaching thunderstorm.  The gustnado was initially reported as a tornado.  No damage was reported nor discovered during a damage survey.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported a woman injured when lightning struck power lines and went through a video game system she was playing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms and heavy rainfall affected northeastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Plumas County Sheriff's Department reported that a woman was struck by lightning at her home in Portola.  She was transported by helicopter to the U.C. Davis Medical Center.  She never regained consciousness and died from her injuries on June 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong Pacific storm system brought a period of strong south to southwest winds to the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  A few high wind gusts were recorded in areas of favorable terrain orientation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 3 miles SE of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another late-season storm brought heavy rainfall to the central Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over Nevada County caused river and creek levels to rise. Some 24-hour rainfall totals included:||5 miles WNW of Truckee, 2.57 inches of rain caused minor street flooding; |Donner Lake HADS site 5 miles west of Truckee (elev. 5924 ft), 1.87 inches; and |Independence Creek SNOTEL (elev. 6500 ft), 1.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms occurred across the eastern Sierra and western Nevada the afternoon and evening of May 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported 1-inch diameter hail in Cromberg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms occurred across the eastern Sierra and western Nevada the afternoon and evening of May 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-size hail was reported 2 miles south of Sierraville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large swells began to affect the coast on Thanksgiving Day causing the surf to increase through Saturday.  Wave heights at the beaches of 14 to 18 feet accompany these large swells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sailboat in rough waters just south of Pillar Point capsized causing two men to enter the ocean. One man was rescued quickly while the other was not removed from the water until an hour after the mishap.  He was pronounced dead by local paramedics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm brought widespread strong winds to northeast and eastern California on November 20. The storm also produced a modest snowfall from around Lake Tahoe northward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walker RAWS recorded a 78 mph wind gust at 1400PST. Other high wind gust observations include: Bridgeport AWOS with 60 mph at 1500PST and the Mammoth Airport AWOS with 68 mph at 2110PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large swells began to affect the coast on Thanksgiving Day causing the surf to increase through Saturday.  Wave heights at the beaches of 14 to 18 feet accompany these large swells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Choppy sea conditions and foggy weather were blamed for causing a wooden-hulled 20-foot boat to capsize near Bodega Bay.  An elderly husband and wife team had launched their motorized boat near Salmon Creek around 6:30 in the morning.  Several hours later their bodies were discovered, one near the broken bow of their boat, the other about one mile south of Salmon Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and intense upper low dropped from British Columbia into western Oregon on the 6th and into northern California on the 7th.  This brought heavy snow to the Sierra along with very strong winds over ridges and into the lower elevations of Mono County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports ranged from 6 to 13 inches.  Here are some specific snowfall reports: 13 inches in Portola, 7-9 inches in Susanville, and 7 inches in Doyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and intense upper low dropped from British Columbia into western Oregon on the 6th and into northern California on the 7th.  This brought heavy snow to the Sierra along with very strong winds over ridges and into the lower elevations of Mono County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm featured an intense, fairly short period of snowfall which affected areas mainly west of highway 395 and near the Sierra Crest with much less snowfall along and east of highway 395. Here are some snowfall totals: An estimated 24 inches at the Leavitt Lake SNOTEL, 13-16 at the Mammoth Lakes Ski Area and Mammoth Pass. Lesser amounts further east include the Mammoth Lakes Ranger Station with 10 inches and the Bridgeport CO-OP with 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moved south along the west coast of California causing damaging winds in portions of the state's northern and central coast on Saturday. Over 180,000 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds brought trees and tree branches down onto vehicles, into streets and onto power lines causing damage across the city of Napa. Down power lines sparked a vehicle fire near Jordan Lane and Lincoln Avenue and an entertainment center on the front porch of a home on the 100 block of Reed Circle caught fire. A Washington Pine tree sheared off and landed in a swimming pool.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moved south along the west coast of California causing damaging winds in portions of the state's northern and central coast on Saturday. Over 180,000 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds knocked trees and power lines down in the Santa Cruz Mountains. Reports of power outages and road closures due to down trees and power lines occurred in the San Lorenzo Valley, Bonny Doon and the Summit areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure center off the coast of Northern California produced a few light showers over the area on August 3rd and 4th. During the third week of the month, monsoonal flow produced scattered showers and thunderstorms. The highest rainfall amounts for the month, mainly due to isolated thunderstorms, were under one-quarter inch. The State of Emergency for California remained in place, as well as previously enforced water restrictions for agricultural and residential areas. No water shortages have been reported. By the end of August, the entire area was classified as D2 (Severe Drought) by the Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor.

An upper-level trough brought an unseasonably cold airmass to central California for the last days of September. High temperatures in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on the 29th were as much as 23 degrees cooler than the previous day. As the cold front moved through the region on the 29th, winds gusted to 40 mph at Hanford, and to 52 mph at Mojave. Winds were also gusty at the base of the Grapevine in the far southern San Joaquin Valley, where winds measured at the CHP Weigh Station gusted to around 35 mph during the afternoon of the 29th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding occurred on Cedar Canyon Road near Lanfair Road.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY SAN FERNANDO VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful early-season storm dropped into the Great Basin. Although this storm did not bring any rainfall to Southwestern California, it did produce very strong and gusty northerly winds. In the mountains of Los Angeles and Ventura counties, north winds gusting to 81 MPH were reported. The strong wind even filtered down to the valleys of Los Angeles county with wind gusts between 58 and 63 MPH reported while stations in the Antelope Valley reported sustained winds between 45 and 50 MPH. The strong winds did knock down some power lines and trees, producing numerous electric outages across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in the San Fernando Valley reported northerly winds gusting to 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge built into California beginning May 27th, bringing another warming trend to interior central California. Fresno warmed to 99 degrees on the 27th, and reached 102 the next day. A weak low off Baja California spun mid-level moisture into California from the south, and the combination of heat and moisture triggered strong thunderstorms over the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains each afternoon and evening, beginning on May 27th and continuing through the end of the month. Hail up to 1.5 inch in diameter fell near the town of Orange Cove on the evening of the 28th, and as thunderstorms over the Sierra Nevada, and over San Luis Obispo County, collapsed, outflow winds pushed across the San Joaquin Valley, triggering thunderstorms over the eastern and central Valley. Two of the storms merged into a strong system that produced gusty winds that toppled numerous trees on the Valley floor, as well as 1-inch hail near Visalia. Strong winds also triggered a gust-front tornado (gustnado) that damaged a mobile home park in Orosi.||Convection was weaker on May 29th, as thunderstorms developed earlier and several of the storms were pushed north of the region by a more southeasterly flow aloft. However, this southeast flow also carried thunderstorms from northeastern Los Angeles and northwestern San Bernardino Counties into southeastern Kern County near Rosamond, Edwards AFB, and Boron. One thunderstorm collapsed as it passed west of Edwards AFB in the early afternoon, generating outflow winds that were measured at a peak of 62 knots (71 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported a large tree downed onto a house from severe thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While high pressure was strengthening over the West Coast, an upper-level low off Baja California began spinning subtropical moisture into California from the southeast. A few thunderstorms developed over the Southern Sierra Nevada on the 17th, but convection was widespread across the region the next day. By the afternoon of May 18th, thunderstorms had developed over the Kern County desert near Edwards Air Force Base, and over extreme southeastern San Luis Obispo County. The latter storm drifted north into the far western portion of Kern County. Four Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued during the afternoon; two verified, including the storm near Edwards Air Force Base. There was a lull in the activity around sunset, then an upper-level disturbance moved northwest along the Southern Sierra Nevada, triggering another round of thunderstorms. The strongest storms were over the mountains of Madera and Mariposa Counties, and as these thunderstorms collapsed, outflow winds caused more storms to develop. Ultimately, the thunderstorms propagated westward into Merced County before finally ending late in the evening. Rainfall from these storms mostly was under a tenth of an inch, although Yosemite Valley recorded 0.33 inch, with a quarter inch of rain falling in one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow generated severe wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May began with a strong upper-level trough moving into California, bringing locally heavy showers to the region. The trough tapped into a deep fetch of subtropical moisture, resulting in high snow levels���above 9000 feet for much of the event, and ultimately no lower than 8200 feet. Fresno received 0.40 inch of rain on May 1st, more than the normal of 0.39 inch for the entire month. Bakersfield received nearly as much, with the rain gauge at Meadows Field measuring 0.35 inch; the normal for the month of May at Bakersfield is 0.24 inch. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, Yosemite Valley received just over 2 inches of rain, and over 1.8 inch of rain fell as far south as Lodgepole in Sequoia National Park. In the high country of the Southern Sierra Nevada, snow did fall, with up to 15 inches at Upper Burnt Corral (9,700 feet). The rain tapered off on May 2nd, as a weak short-wave move through the region behind the main trough, and only a few showers lingered into the 3rd.

The latter storm drifted north into the far western portion of Kern County. Four Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued during the afternoon; two verified, including the storm near Edwards Air Force Base. There was a lull in the activity around sunset, then an upper-level disturbance moved northwest along the Southern Sierra Nevada, triggering another round of thunderstorms. The strongest storms were over the mountains of Madera and Mariposa Counties, and as these thunderstorms collapsed, outflow winds caused more storms to develop. Ultimately, the thunderstorms propagated westward into Merced County before finally ending late in the evening. Rainfall from these storms mostly was under a tenth of an inch, although Yosemite Valley recorded 0.33 inch, with a quarter inch of rain falling in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From October 12th through October 14th, the first significant storm of the season moved through Southern California. The storm brought heavy rainfall and very gusty southerly winds to the area. Rainfall totals were very impressive, generally ranging between 1 and 3 inches over coastal areas to between 4 and 10 inches in the mountains. Some isolated areas received rainfall amounts over 10 inches over San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties. This storm brought very strong and gusty southerly winds to the area with warning-level winds reported in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty southeast winds were reported across the mountains of Los Angeles county. The Camp Nine RAWS sensor reported a wind gust of 69 mph while the Grass Mountain RAWS sensor reported wind gusts to 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Red Rock fire in the Marble Mountain Wilderness portion of the Klamath National Forest was started by humans on 8/21/09 around 7 PM PDT. As of 09/01/09, it was at 1382 acres and was 85 percent contained.  Fire fighting efforts were transferred to a local unit on 09/01/09, and full containment is estimated to occur on 9/5/9.  As of 09/01/09, the cost of the fire was 3.7 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Rock fire in the Marble Mountain Wilderness portion of the Klamath National Forest was started by humans on 8/21/09 around 7 PM PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Harrington fire in the Marble Mountain Wilderness portion of the Klamath National Forest was started by lightning on 8/02/09 around 5 PM PDT. As of 09/01/09, it was at 142 acres and was 100 percent contained.  As of 09/01/09, the cost of the fire was 482,000 dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harrington fire in the Marble Mountain Wilderness portion of the Klamath National Forest was started by lightning on 8/02/09 around 5 PM PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Station Wildfire started on August 26th in the mountains of Los Angeles County in the Angeles National Forest. The fire burned intensely into the middle of September. When the fire was contained, it had burned over 160,000 acres and destroyed numerous homes. In addition, two Los Angeles County firefighters were killed when evacuating their fire station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Station Wildfire burned 160,577 acres across the mountains of Los Angeles County in the Angeles National Forest from late August through mid-September. The suspected cause of the fire was arson. The Station Fire destroyed 209 structures, including 89 residences. In addition, two Los Angeles County firefighters were killed on August 30th while evacuating their fire camp. The total suppression cost for the fire was over $93 million.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS officials toured damage around the town of Orosi in Tulare County. Extensive wind damage from thunderstorm outflow in the town was evident. Several homes exhibited minor roof damage. Fences and trees were damaged or downed. Property damages were estimated. NWS concluded that a gust front tornado, commonly called a gustnado, occurred in this location, based on damage pattern as well as patterns indicated in tall dry grass and tree trunks and tops, and eyewitness accounts. Gust front tornadoes can occur in the outflow of a severe thunderstorm and are not considered to be true tornadoes. Winds were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge built into California beginning May 27th, bringing another warming trend to interior central California. Fresno warmed to 99 degrees on the 27th, and reached 102 the next day. A weak low off Baja California spun mid-level moisture into California from the south, and the combination of heat and moisture triggered strong thunderstorms over the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains each afternoon and evening, beginning on May 27th and continuing through the end of the month. Hail up to 1.5 inch in diameter fell near the town of Orange Cove on the evening of the 28th, and as thunderstorms over the Sierra Nevada, and over San Luis Obispo County, collapsed, outflow winds pushed across the San Joaquin Valley, triggering thunderstorms over the eastern and central Valley. Two of the storms merged into a strong system that produced gusty winds that toppled numerous trees on the Valley floor, as well as 1-inch hail near Visalia. Strong winds also triggered a gust-front tornado (gustnado) that damaged a mobile home park in Orosi.||Convection was weaker on May 29th, as thunderstorms developed earlier and several of the storms were pushed north of the region by a more southeasterly flow aloft. However, this southeast flow also carried thunderstorms from northeastern Los Angeles and northwestern San Bernardino Counties into southeastern Kern County near Rosamond, Edwards AFB, and Boron. One thunderstorm collapsed as it passed west of Edwards AFB in the early afternoon, generating outflow winds that were measured at a peak of 62 knots (71 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained SKYWARN weather spotter reported numerous windshields broken in the Coarsegold area and along SR 41 near the Chukchansi Report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough brought an unseasonably cold airmass to central California for the last days of September. High temperatures in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on the 29th were as much as 23 degrees cooler than the previous day. As the cold front moved through the region on the 29th, winds gusted to 40 mph at Hanford, and to 52 mph at Mojave. Winds were also gusty at the base of the Grapevine in the far southern San Joaquin Valley, where winds measured at the CHP Weigh Station gusted to around 35 mph during the afternoon of the 29th.

A few wildfires continued during the month of September in the Sierra Nevada.  ||The Power fire in Sequoia National Forest, ignited on September 27th early in the morning. The cause was unknown. The location was Highway 190 near Springville in Tulare County. The size was 400 acres. The fire was contained on September 29th at a cost of $500,000.
A woman died of heat-related causes in Death Valley National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of seven tourists walked out into a dunes area in Death Valley National Park during the heat of the day.  As they returned to their vehicles, one collapsed and died.  Three others were treated for heat-related illnesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure center off the coast of Northern California produced a few light showers over the area on August 3rd and 4th. During the third week of the month, monsoonal flow produced scattered showers and thunderstorms. The highest rainfall amounts for the month, mainly due to isolated thunderstorms, were under one-quarter inch. The State of Emergency for California remained in place, as well as previously enforced water restrictions for agricultural and residential areas. No water shortages have been reported. By the end of August, the entire area was classified as D2 (Severe Drought) by the Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Distant storms produced very long period waves on the northern California coast.  These waves produced a near-shore marine hazard due to the shoaling and set behavior of long period waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fisherman drowned when his small fishing vessel was capsized by the set behavior of long period waves.  The hazard was enhanced by the shoaling of these waves as they entered the shallow water that the vessel was operating in.  The waves were 12.5 feet high with a period of 20 seconds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and monsoon moisture combined to trigger an outbreak of thunderstorms across the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of water and debris flowed across Highway 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front helped lift deep subtropical moisture seeping north from the remnants of Hurricane Jimena, triggering severe storms and flash flooding in the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain washed debris onto I-15 between Bailey Rd. and Nipton Rd.  The Highway Patrol had to escort vehicles through.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris flowed across Nipton Rd. at the Nipton Trading Post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May began with a strong upper-level trough moving into California, bringing locally heavy showers to the region. The trough tapped into a deep fetch of subtropical moisture, resulting in high snow levels���above 9000 feet for much of the event, and ultimately no lower than 8200 feet. Fresno received 0.40 inch of rain on May 1st, more than the normal of 0.39 inch for the entire month. Bakersfield received nearly as much, with the rain gauge at Meadows Field measuring 0.35 inch; the normal for the month of May at Bakersfield is 0.24 inch. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, Yosemite Valley received just over 2 inches of rain, and over 1.8 inch of rain fell as far south as Lodgepole in Sequoia National Park. In the high country of the Southern Sierra Nevada, snow did fall, with up to 15 inches at Upper Burnt Corral (9,700 feet). The rain tapered off on May 2nd, as a weak short-wave move through the region behind the main trough, and only a few showers lingered into the 3rd.

A low pressure trough moved into central California on May 5th, bringing only light precipitation to the Southern Sierra Nevada. This trough did, however, tighten surface pressure gradients across the region, producing gusts to 52 mph at River Kern and sustained winds of 32 mph at Edwards AFB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a period of several hours brought local flooding to portions of Redding as small streams rose quickly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Redding Police reported Boulder Creek running out of its banks, flooding an adjacent RV park and apartment complex.  Evacuations were ordered and minor damage was done to several RVs. Clogged storm drains resulted in the flooding of a nearby intersection to nearly three feet deep, in which several cars stalled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from the Gulf of Alaska to the Desert Southwest, bringing high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at Twentynine Palms (KNXP).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Oak Glen III Fire started on August 30th around 1:46 PM PDT near the community of Oak Glen in the San Bernardino National Forest. Mandatory evacuations were ordered for parts of Oak Glen. The fire burned 1,159 acres before being contained on September 8th. Approximately 2,000 homes were threatened. One residence and one outbuilding were destroyed, and one other residence was damaged. Four injuries were reported, though the details are unknown. The fire cost approximately 6.9 million dollars to fight.

The Pendleton Fire began at approximately 3:30 PM PDT on August 31st near Yucaipa. Evacuations were ordered for 1,100 homes and some roads were closed. One residential structure and one outbuilding were destroyed in the 860 acre fire, which was contained on September 3rd. One injury was reported. The cause of the 1.49 million dollar fire is still under investigation.

Thunderstorms on the 2nd and 5th of the month produced heavy rain in some desert, mountain, and valley locations, with isolated rainfall totals over one inch. The remainder of the month was relatively dry. ||California remained in a State of Emergency for the ongoing drought. Water restrictions and voluntary conservation remained in force, as well as water cut-backs for agricultural purposes. On September 17th, the U.S. Department of Agriculture granted a Secretarial disaster designation for many counties in California, including Orange, Riverside, San Bernardino, and San Diego. This was due to agricultural losses caused by drought beginning on January 1, 2009.||The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor continued a D2 (Severe Drought) for the mountains, valleys, and coastal regions, while the deserts remained in D1 (Moderate Drought).
A system moving west to east between 35 and 40 north latitude brought a heavy, wet snow to eastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 15 inches of snow fell in the higher terrain from Grizzly Ridge (NW of Portola) southward. Lesser amounts fell further north and east, including: Portola with 6 inches and Susanville with 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system moving west to east between 35 and 40 north latitude brought a heavy, wet snow to eastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In general, 1 to 2 feet of snow fell north and west of Lake Tahoe with 8 to 15 inches elsewhere, much of it from midday on the 12th through early morning on the 13th (12-16 hours). Here are some specific snowfall totals: 28 inches at the Northstar and Sugarbowl Ski areas, 20 inches at the Heavenly Ski Resort, 16 inches in Tahoe City and Truckee, and 9 inches at the South Lake Tahoe airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system moving west to east between 35 and 40 north latitude brought a heavy, wet snow to eastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen to 28 inches of snowfall was reported with this system, mainly in the higher terrain west of highway 395, with 10 to 15 inches along highway 395 from Bridgeport to Mammoth Lakes. Specific snowfall tallies include: 28 inches one mile WNW of Mammoth Lakes, 24 inches at the Mammoth Ski Area, 15 inches at the Mammoth Lakes Ranger Station, and 10 inches at the Lee Vining CO-OP station.||In addition to heavy snowfall, sporadic gusty winds of 30-45 mph were recorded along highway 395 from Bridgeport south with an incredible 164 mph gust at the Mammoth Ski Area at 9950 feet (1900PST on the 12th).

Remnants of a typhoon enhanced very moist flow off the Pacific Ocean and brought strong winds (to 135 mph over the Sierra Crest) and unusually heavy to record-setting rainfall to the region. An average of 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rainfall fell immediately east of the Sierra with 3 to 5 inches (locally as high as 6-7 inches) in the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wind gust was measured by a trained spotter 3 miles west of Loyalton.  The spotter also reported winds sustained at 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sheep Fire started along Sheep Creek Road near Lytle Creek around 1311 PST on October 3rd. The fire spread rapidly towards Wrightwood, scorching a total of 7,128 acres before being contained on October 10th. The fire forced approximately 5,000 residents to evacuate and destroyed one residence, four other structures, five cars, four RVs, and 2 pieces of heavy equipment. Eight firefighters were injured. The fire cost over eight million dollars to fight. Investigators say the fire was started by a human, but it is still unclear whether it was intentional or not.

A large winter storm centered off the Oregon coast brought light precipitation on the 13th and 14th. Some areas in San Bernardino County received one to three inches of rain, while the rest of the region had one-quarter inch or less. The State of Emergency for the State of California, as well as the Secretarial Disaster Declaration, remained in place. Some mandatory and voluntary water restrictions remained in place as well. The Weekly U.S. Drought Monitor had all areas except for the deserts classified as D2 (severe drought), while the deserts remained at D1 (moderate drought).
