EVENT_NARRATIVE: An overloaded electric transformer failed in Oakland causing more than 5,000 customers to lose power for about three hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft centered over Reno, NV along with weak offshore flow at the surface caused temperatures to rise to near 100 degrees in the inland valleys of north-central California. Heat exhausted individuals, blown electric transformers and power outages accompanied the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several people sought medical attention due to heat-induced fainting spells in Anitoch and Walnut Creek. Also, an electric transformer failed sparking a fire in Walnut Creek near the intersection of Wiget Lane and Ygnacio Valley Road causing 108 customers to lose power. The fire damaged nearby vegetation and an olive tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around July 12th, a strong high pressure ridge began to build west into California, and strengthened over the next several days. Temperatures quickly warmed to well above normal, with widespread triple-digit high temperatures on the 14th. This warming continued over the next several days, with Fresno reaching a high of 112 degrees on July 19th, and Bakersfield only one degree cooler. Both high temperatures set records for that date. Similar recordings were measured around the San Joaquin Valley, adjacent Sierra Foothills, and the Western Mojave Desert. Additionally, temperatures in the urban centers of Bakersfield and Fresno struggled to cool below 80 degrees on the mornings of July 18-20.

An upper-level disturbance off the west coast of Baja California spun a fetch of subtropical moisture into California beginning July 16th, with isolated thunderstorms developing along the Southern Sierra Nevada crest. By the 18th, there was enough moisture that thunderstorms also formed in the Kern County deserts, with one afternoon thunderstorm near Edwards AFB reaching severe criteria with gusts to 67 mph. Thunderstorms over the Sierra and the Kern desert redeveloped the next day, and spread into the Tehachapi Mountains. Around an inch of rain fell in the Frazier Park-Pine Mountain Club area, but no flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter at Pine Mountain Club reported heavy rainfall of about one inch.

The Elephant Fire burned an area of forest along Feather River Canyon, near Rock Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elephant Wildfire burned 445 acres of woodland.  Highway 70 travel was limited at one point to one way traffic with a pilot car, causing travel delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm system dropped southward over interior Northern California, bringing heavy snow to the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains and locally significant accumulations down onto the Central Valley floor.  ||Widespread and numerous car accidents, power outages, and school closures resulted from the storm and large stretches of roads and highways were impacted by the low level snow, with many closures and long delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains, with up to 3 feet of accumulation at the highest elevations.  The heavy snow along with gusty winds produced hazardous road conditions in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm system dropped southward over interior Northern California, bringing heavy snow to the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains and locally significant accumulations down onto the Central Valley floor.  ||Widespread and numerous car accidents, power outages, and school closures resulted from the storm and large stretches of roads and highways were impacted by the low level snow, with many closures and long delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain turned to snow overnight as the snow level lowered steadily through the event.  By morning, eighteen inches of snow had fallen in Placerville.  Power was knocked out throughout the area and remained out for several days in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought one to two feet of snow and strong winds to the higher elevations of the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two feet of snow fell at the highest elevations and winds to 50 mph brought visibilities down to a mile or less in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving winter storm brought another round of snow to the far northern Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought up to a foot of snow to the higher elevations.

The Hat Creek Complex and SHU Lightning Complex fires were started by lightning strikes from thunderstorms on the evening of August 1st.  Approximately 1800 lightning ground strikes were recorded in Northern California.  The Hat Creek Complex continued until containment on August 12th.  THe SHU Lightning Complex continued until containment on August 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm lightning strikes started numerous fires in Shasta County on the evening of August 1st. The SHU Complex consisted of 40 fires, with the largest being the Gomez, Cave, Cassel, the Goose, and the Chalk fires.  Dozens of homes were evacuated and a number of area roads were closed. There were 17,623 acres of forest burned prior to containment on August 17th.  This fire complex included burned acreage in CA-Z013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Knight Fire burned an area of forest along the Middle Fork of the American River.  The fire started on July 26th and was 100 percent contained by August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 6130 acres of forest burned in rugged terrain along the Middle Fork of the American River.  Local forest service roads were closed due to the fire, as were several campgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hat Creek Complex and SHU Lightning Complex fires were started by lightning strikes from thunderstorms on the evening of August 1st.  Approximately 1800 lightning ground strikes were recorded in Northern California.  The Hat Creek Complex continued until containment on August 12th.  THe SHU Lightning Complex continued until containment on August 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm lightning strikes starting at least 43 fires in Lassen National Forest on August 1st. The largest of the Hat Creek Complex fires were the Sugarloaf, Brown, and the Butte fires. There were 11,269 acres of forest burned in the Complex.  Other impacts included evacuations of campgrounds in the Hat Creek Recreation area, road closures of portions of State Highway 89 and State Highway 44, and the closure of the Pacific Crest Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms occurred over the central Sierra during the afternoon of July 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm downdraft damaged two aircraft at the Soar Truckee facility at the Truckee-Tahoe Airport.  The wind gust destroyed an SGS 2-33 aircraft and damaged another glider.  No gliders were airborne at the time and no one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system on the coast coupled with an unstable atmosphere brought isolated thunderstorms over the mountains of interior northern California. Hail was reported locally in western Plumas County each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lassen Volcanic National Park rangers reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system on the coast coupled with an unstable atmosphere brought isolated thunderstorms over the mountains of interior northern California. Hail was reported locally in western Plumas County each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Co-operative observer estimated hail from dime to penny sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Knight Fire started on July 26th, burning a forested area by the Middle Fork of the Stanislaus River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started on July 26th along the Middle Fork of the Stanislaus River, near Mt. Knight, in Stanislaus County.  The fire had burned 2734 acres by the end of July and continued to burn into August.  It brought multiple local road closures and smoke to the nearby area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boy died in a remote corner of Death Valley National Park after being stranded with his mother for four days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boy died in the extreme southwest corner of Death Valley National Park after being stranded with his mother for four days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long period four foot south swell generated high surf and strong rip currents along the beaches of extreme Southern California. Eight to ten foot surf with sets to twelve feet were observed at many South facing beaches, and sets up to twenty feet reported in the most favorable locations. The high surf also generated strong rip currents, which were responsible for hundreds, perhaps even thousands, of rescues throughout Southern California. Structural damage and one death was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported surf up to 20 feet at The Wedge in Newport Beach on Friday the 24th. Around 1300 PDT, a body surfer was killed when large waves and strong currents forced him into a nearby rock jetty. The cause of death was reported to be drowning. Further south, a Dana Point sheriff's deputy sustained a back injury when his patrol boat went airborne after it crested an unanticipated wave in the open water. In San Clemente, a portion of the fishing pier was closed after high surf tore off a wooden crossbeam. The pier reopened at 1100 PDT Saturday. Hundreds of rip current rescues were reported at various beaches in Orange County as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 5th, copious low-level moisture over the deserts combined with extensive daytime heating and an upper-level trough triggered a massive severe thunderstorm over the San Diego County deserts just south of Ocotillo Wells. Surface dew point temperatures in the area were as high as 75 degrees, with air temperatures reaching 105 degrees. According to radar data, storm tops were at least 60 kft. The storm, which was nearly stationary and lasted for over 90 minutes, produced flash flooding, damaging wind, and large hail. No hail size reports were received, but based on radar data it is likely that at least golf ball sized hail was being produced by this storm. Strong downdrafts knocked over several power poles along Split Mountain Road. Radar data also indicated a rather impressive anti-cyclonic mesocyclone and strong low-level rotation, with up to 106 kt gate-to-gate shear around 4800 ft AGL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm with heavy rain near the town of Ocotillo Wells caused flash flooding along the Fish Creek Wash and Split Mountain Road. Several power poles were down in the area, but this may have been attributed to high winds as well as the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two wildland fires broke out in Norco On September 22. The fire in the hills east of the city burned 160 acres destroyed a storage building, while the 85 acre fire in the Santa Ana River bottom caused minor damage to one home. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire broke out just after 1200 PDT in the hills between Norco and Riverside just East of the Interstate 15 between Fourth and Fifth streets. The Fire consumed 150 acres and destroyed a storage building.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second fire began around 1330 PDT in the Santa Ana River bottom near Norco. The fire burned 85 acres and caused minor damage to one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few wildfires continued during the month of September in the Sierra Nevada.  ||The Wildcat wildfire, which ignited on July 29, 2009, continued to burn during the months of August and September. The location was 9 miles northwest of Tuolumne Meadows in Yosemite NP. The original cause was from lightning. The size was 1255 acres. ||The Power fire in Sequoia National Forest, ignited on September 27th early in the morning. The cause was unknown. The location was Highway 190 near Springville in Tulare County. The size was 400 acres. The fire was contained on September 29th at a cost of $500,000.

A low pressure system tapped into sub-tropical moisture causing hundreds of lightning strikes in thunderstorms around the San Francisco Bay Area, California.  Nearly 50,000 Bay Area residents lost power and dozens of small fires were started.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house and surrounding trees in the 300 block of Summit Avenue damaging two 200-foot-tall Redwood trees and a driveway.  Also the lightning fried the television and a video game system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tapped into sub-tropical moisture causing hundreds of lightning strikes in thunderstorms around the San Francisco Bay Area, California.  Nearly 50,000 Bay Area residents lost power and dozens of small fires were started.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 47,000 residents lost power around San Francisco as thunderstorms rolled through the city/county.  The largest outage affected 36,000 customers in parts of Sunset, Glen Park and Park Merced neighborhoods.  One lightning strike hit a power transformer causing it to explode.  Besides the total destruction of the power transformer, two vehicles were damaged at the intersection of Broderick and Sacramento Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused power outages and damage to power lines across San Mateo County as tree limbs crashed into them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 270 customers lost power in Pescadero as strong and gusty winds blew tree limbs onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air brought freezing temperatures to Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures ranged from 31 to 39 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air brought freezing temperatures to Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures ranged from 27 to 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought about 1500 recorded lightning strikes to northern California.  One lightning strike injured a woman at a sporting event in Stockton and a number of small wildfires were started across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a number of fires over the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains including the Telegraph Fire near Copperopolis (200 acres) and the San Antonio Fire near Arnold (5 acres), both located in Calaveras County.  The other fires were small and quickly extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northeastern California and western Nevada on the 29th.  Strong winds ahead of the front caused damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bridgeport RAWS recorded a wind gust of 65 mph while the Walker RAWS recorded a wind gust to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Oasis Fire burned in the Cache Creek Wilderness in eastern Lake County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oasis Fire burned 1575 acres in the Cache Creek Wilderness in eastern Lake County.  There were 4 firefighters injured, but no structures damaged or destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Silver Fire burned in the Plumas National Forest in Plumas County, about 10 miles west of Quincy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver Fire burned 307 acres of forest in steep terrain in the Plumas National Forest in Plumas County.  The fire burned to about 10 miles west of Quincy and was also near Silver Lake and the community of Meadow Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front followed by strong cold air advection lowered snow levels between 2000-3000 feet which allowed for 6 to 6.5 inches of snow to be observed within a 12 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter measured 6.5 inches of snow in a 12 hour period. This report came in at 20/1719 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front followed by strong cold air advection lowered snow levels between 2000-3000 feet which allowed for 6 to 6.5 inches of snow to be observed within a 12 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Snowman Summit rain gage received 0.91 inches of liquid equivalent in 7 hours. Temperatures were near freezing during the time so an assumption of at least 6 inches of snow was made based on the meteorological conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought about 1500 recorded lightning strikes to northern California.  One lightning strike injured a woman at a sporting event in Stockton and a number of small wildfires were started across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was struck by lightning while carrying a large umbrella at a softball game at the San Joaquin Regional Sports Complex in Stockton.  Additional reports suggest the lightning hit about 30 feet away from her.  The woman suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Super Typhoon Melor from the western Pacific Ocean combined with a Canadian upper level low pressure system to form a strong storm over Northern California. Numerous trees and large branches were knocked down, causing damage to cars, homes, and power lines which resulted in the loss of power to thousands of customers. Heavy rain caused minor flooding on roads and in small streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various locations in Calaveras County reported 4 to 6 inches of rain on the 13th.  This resulted in numerous storm drains becoming clogged which caused local street flooding.||Strong, gusty winds brought down large numbers of trees, large branches, and power lines which resulted in power outages to many customers in Calaveras County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Super Typhoon Melor from the western Pacific Ocean combined with a Canadian upper level low pressure system to form a strong storm over Northern California. Numerous trees and large branches were knocked down, causing damage to cars, homes, and power lines which resulted in the loss of power to thousands of customers. Heavy rain caused minor flooding on roads and in small streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stirling City recorded 4.96 inches of rainfall on the 13th and Chico recorded 1.35 inches.  The heavy rain caused numerous storm drains to clog which resulted in local street flooding.||Strong, gusty winds brought down large numbers of trees, large branches, and power lines which resulted in power outages to at least 2,600 customers in Butte County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty winds to eastern California followed by a period of lake effect snow which brought several inches of snowfall to the south and southeast of Lake Tahoe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union newspaper in Grass Valley stated that the Coast Guard on Lake Tahoe responded to three capsized boats.  Buoys on Lake Tahoe recorded sustained winds of 35-40 mph with a few brief periods as high as 40-45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow behind a cold front brought gusty winds to the mountains and deserts of Southern California on November 27th. The wind blew down several eucalyptus trees and caused a few power outages in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured gusts in excess of 58 MPH between 0100 and 0130 PST along Montezuma Grade Road in Ranchita. At 0109 PST, a peak gust of 60 MPH was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow behind a cold front brought gusty winds to the mountains and deserts of Southern California on November 27th. The wind blew down several eucalyptus trees and caused a few power outages in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in Whitewater between 0950 and 1050 PST, during which time a peak gust of 68 MPH was measured by the Whitewater RAWS. High winds ended between 1250 and 1350 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow behind a cold front brought gusty winds to the mountains and deserts of Southern California on November 27th. The wind blew down several eucalyptus trees and caused a few power outages in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting around 30 MPH caused a eucalyptus tree to fall on a car driving along Rancheros Drive in San Marcos. The tree trapped the driver and one passenger in the car. The couple sustained only minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm brought widespread strong winds to northeast and eastern California on November 20. The storm also produced a modest snowfall from around Lake Tahoe northward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overall, most of the area received 3 inches or less of snowfall on November 20.  However, isolated areas of Plumas and Sierra Counties received heavier amounts including 4 inches in Portola, 3.8 inches 3 miles SSW of Susanville, and approximately 6 inches 12 miles NW of Portola (on a ridge at 6900 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm brought widespread strong winds to northeast and eastern California on November 20. The storm also produced a modest snowfall from around Lake Tahoe northward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense but brief period of snow dropped modest snowfall amounts.  Here are some of the snowfall totals: 6-10 inches near the Sierra crest west of Lake Tahoe, Tahoe City with 5-8 inches, 5 inches for Truckee and South Lake Tahoe, and an estimated 3-6 inches from SNOTEL observations south and east of Lake Tahoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Super Typhoon Melor from the western Pacific Ocean combined with a Canadian upper level low pressure system to form a strong storm over Northern California. Numerous trees and large branches were knocked down, causing damage to cars, homes, and power lines which resulted in the loss of power to thousands of customers. Heavy rain caused minor flooding on roads and in small streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downtown Sacramento recorded 3.04 inches of rain on the 13th, which was the second highest 24 hour rain amount to occur in the month of October and the 11th highest of all time.  An additional 0.20 inches fell on the 14th.  Other towns in the vicinity received two to three inches for the storm total.||Numerous storm drains clogged, causing local street flooding.  The most notable was  along the section of Interstate 5 going through downtown Sacramento, which caused travel problems for many hours.  ||Several buildings were damaged from the heavy rain including a Filco store in Citrus Heights that suffered a roof collapse from water buildup. Minor flooding was also reported to have damaged nearby homes.||Peak wind gusts of 48 mph were recorded at Sacramento Executive Airport on the 13th.  Hundreds of trees and large branches fell, damaging numerous cars and causing power outages for approximately 124,000 customers in the Sacramento area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and lift associated with a cold front and low snow levels brought heavy snow  to parts of northern California late in the evening of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowman Summit at 4478 feet reported 0.95 inches of water in roughly a 20 hour period. Air and road temperatures were near freezing. Using a 10 to 1 snow ratio, warning criteria was met at roughly 14/2345 PST. In addition, Mt. Shasta Ski Park reported 8 inches of new snow on the 16th which further backs up that warning criteria levels were met for CAZ082.  The Castle Lake station at 5870 feet reported about 12 inches of snow during the overnight hours as well.  The Mt. Shasta Avalanche Center reported that most of their stations, which are above 6000 feet, reported 13-18 inches of snow, and a rain gage in Mt. Shasta City recorded 2.11 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system made its way through Northern and Central California accompanied by deep tropical moisture and very strong winds.  Heavy rain combined with the wind to cause numerous trees, tree limbs and pole/telephone powers to fall.  Pacific Gas and Electric reported over 277,000 customers had lost power in the San Francisco and Monterey Bay Areas with a cost of over thirteen million dollars in damages.  The record breaking heavy rain also led to flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow closed both lanes of Highway 1 at mile marker 37 around 4:15 p.m. PDT.  Also, some flooding occurred along the Big Sur River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system made its way through Northern and Central California accompanied by deep tropical moisture and very strong winds.  Heavy rain combined with the wind to cause numerous trees, tree limbs and pole/telephone powers to fall.  Pacific Gas and Electric reported over 277,000 customers had lost power in the San Francisco and Monterey Bay Areas with a cost of over thirteen million dollars in damages.  The record breaking heavy rain also led to flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow initiated in a seasonal stream bed rushing hundreds of feet downhill into a driveway and home on Upper Highland Road near Eureka Canyon Road.  A car and one side of a home were buried in about nine feet of rock, dirt, wood chunks and water.  The home was severely damaged and deemed unsafe and uninhabitable. Over thirty Santa Cruz county roads were closed and damaged in flash flooding and debris flows in the Santa Cruz Mountains.  Two people were rescued from a vehicle when they got stuck in water along Eureka Canyon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow behind a cold front brought gusty winds to the mountains and deserts of Southern California on November 27th. The wind blew down several eucalyptus trees and caused a few power outages in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the Burns Canyon RAWS between 0851 and 0951 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a typhoon enhanced very moist flow off the Pacific Ocean and brought strong winds (to 135 mph over the Sierra Crest) and unusually heavy to record-setting rainfall to the region. An average of 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rainfall fell immediately east of the Sierra with 3 to 5 inches (locally as high as 6-7 inches) in the Sierra.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the month of August, the La Brea Wildfire burned over 89,000 acres in the Los Padres National Forest in the county of Santa Barbara. The fire was fueled by very hot temperatures, low relative humidity and significant fuels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The La Brea Wildfire burned 89,489 acres of land in the mountains of Santa Barbara county in the Los Padres National Forest. The cause of the fire was determined to be a cooking fire at an illegal marijuana operation in the forest. Fortunately only two structures were destrouyed: a hunter's cabin and a unused ranger's cabin. The main conditions driving this wildfire were hot temperatures, very low relative humidity and a very old and dry fuel bed. Overall, the estimated cost to suppress the fire was $23 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Station Wildfire started in late August in the mountains of Los Angeles County in the Angeles National Forest. The fire burned intensely into the middle of September. When the fire was contained, it had burned over 160,000 acres and destroyed numerous homes. In addition, two Los Angeles County firefighters were killed when evacuating their fire station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Station Wildfire burned 160,577 acres across the mountains of Los Angeles County in the Angeles National Forest from late August through mid-September. The suspected cause of the fire was arson. The Station Fire destroyed 209 structures, including 89 residences. In addition, two Los Angeles County firefighters were killed on August 30th while evacuating their fire camp. The total suppression cost for the fire was over $93 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From October 12th through October 14th, the first significant storm of the season moved through Southern California. The storm brought heavy rainfall and very gusty southerly winds to the area. Rainfall totals were very impressive, generally ranging between 1 and 3 inches over coastal areas to between 4 and 10 inches in the mountains. Some isolated areas received rainfall amounts over 10 inches over San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties. This storm brought very strong and gusty southerly winds to the area with warning-level winds reported in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated sensor near the Grapevine area (northern Ventura county) reported southerly winds gusting to 64 mph.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cottonwood Fire began four miles East of Hemet on August 27th at approximately 5:13 PM PDT. The fire spread East along California Highway 74 towards Idyllwild in the San Bernardino National Forest. The fire burned 2,409 acres before being contained on August 31st. No structures were damaged, and five firefighters sustained heat related injuries. The cause of the fire may have been due to a power pole, but is still under investigation.

The Cottonwood Fire began four miles East of Hemet on August 27th at approximately 5:13 PM PDT. An upper-level ridge built into California beginning May 27th, bringing another warming trend to interior central California. Fresno warmed to 99 degrees on the 27th, and reached 102 the next day. A weak low off Baja California spun mid-level moisture into California from the south, and the combination of heat and moisture triggered strong thunderstorms over the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains each afternoon and evening, beginning on May 27th and continuing through the end of the month. Hail up to 1.5 inch in diameter fell near the town of Orange Cove on the evening of the 28th, and as thunderstorms over the Sierra Nevada, and over San Luis Obispo County, collapsed, outflow winds pushed across the San Joaquin Valley, triggering thunderstorms over the eastern and central Valley. Two of the storms merged into a strong system that produced gusty winds that toppled numerous trees on the Valley floor, as well as 1-inch hail near Visalia. Strong winds also triggered a gust-front tornado (gustnado) that damaged a mobile home park in Orosi.||Convection was weaker on May 29th, as thunderstorms developed earlier and several of the storms were pushed north of the region by a more southeasterly flow aloft. However, this southeast flow also carried thunderstorms from northeastern Los Angeles and northwestern San Bernardino Counties into southeastern Kern County near Rosamond, Edwards AFB, and Boron. One thunderstorm collapsed as it passed west of Edwards AFB in the early afternoon, generating outflow winds that were measured at a peak of 62 knots (71 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained NWS spotter reported ping pong ball sized hail (1.5 inches in diameter) 6 miles east of Orange Cove. The large hail caused broken windows, roof damage, dented metal and damaged windshields on cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system made its way through Northern and Central California accompanied by deep tropical moisture and very strong winds.  Heavy rain combined with the wind to cause numerous trees, tree limbs and pole/telephone powers to fall.  Pacific Gas and Electric reported over 277,000 customers had lost power in the San Francisco and Monterey Bay Areas with a cost of over thirteen million dollars in damages.  The record breaking heavy rain also led to flooding and debris flows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge built into California beginning May 27th, bringing another warming trend to interior central California. Fresno warmed to 99 degrees on the 27th, and reached 102 the next day. A weak low off Baja California spun mid-level moisture into California from the south, and the combination of heat and moisture triggered strong thunderstorms over the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains each afternoon and evening, beginning on May 27th and continuing through the end of the month. Hail up to 1.5 inch in diameter fell near the town of Orange Cove on the evening of the 28th, and as thunderstorms over the Sierra Nevada, and over San Luis Obispo County, collapsed, outflow winds pushed across the San Joaquin Valley, triggering thunderstorms over the eastern and central Valley. Two of the storms merged into a strong system that produced gusty winds that toppled numerous trees on the Valley floor, as well as 1-inch hail near Visalia. Strong winds also triggered a gust-front tornado (gustnado) that damaged a mobile home park in Orosi.||Convection was weaker on May 29th, as thunderstorms developed earlier and several of the storms were pushed north of the region by a more southeasterly flow aloft. However, this southeast flow also carried thunderstorms from northeastern Los Angeles and northwestern San Bernardino Counties into southeastern Kern County near Rosamond, Edwards AFB, and Boron. One thunderstorm collapsed as it passed west of Edwards AFB in the early afternoon, generating outflow winds that were measured at a peak of 62 knots (71 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported a large tree downed in the town of Sanger.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds across San Francisco brought numerous trees down and caused thousands of buildings to lose power. On Hyde Street near Green Street a parked car was crushed as a three-story high eucalyptus tree fell onto it while a truck just pulling away received a cracked windshield. At the San Francisco State University campus a few trees were uprooted near Mary Park and Mary Ward halls. Elsewhere in the city a tree fell across the entire span of Broadway between Powell and Stockton Streets.  Over 125 calls to the Department of Public Works regarding tree issues occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monterey Road between Third and Fourth Streets was submerged due to flooding.  Flood waters entered a restaurant on Fourth Street causing damage.  Streets were flooded throughout Morgan Hill with water as high as two feet is some locations. The city actually ran out of moveable flooded signs and barriers, unable to mark all flooded locations.  Here are some of the locations experiencing flooding:  south end of town near the Morgan Hill Post Office on Monterey Road; Fountain Avenue; Llagas Creek Road, Monterey Road north of Morgan Hill, near Cochrane Road; Old Monterey Road from Monterey to Llagas Roads; Butterfield Boulevard at several locations, including at San Pedro and Diana Avenues; Watsonville Road at Monterey Road; Monterey Road at Burnett Avenue; Tennant Avenue; and Wright Avenue from Del Monte to Hale Avenues. Also, a sewage pipe connecting Morgan Hill to the Gilroy sewage treatment plant backed up causing 40,000 gallons of raw sewage to spill into the Ludewig Ranch causing the cancellation of the Harvest Festival in San Martin, an event to raise funds and food for the homeless.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40-foot acacia tree toppled onto a garage roof in Campbell on West Rincon Avenue. The 30-year-old tree became wedged between the roof and the second story and blocked the entire entrance to the home.  At the Blossom Hill Elementary School in Los Gatos, a row of young trees were broken and bent lying on the ground due to the strong wind. At the intersection of Highway 17 and Interstate 280 a tree fell blocking a lane of traffic. In Milpitas, a large tree landed on a house on the 1300 block of Lassen Avenue clipping on side of the house causing minor gutter and roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Guiberson Wildfire burned over 17,000 acres in the valleys of Ventura County. The fire itself was driven by a moderate Santa Ana wind event with gusty northeast winds to around 50 mph reported in and near the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Guiberson Wildfire burned 17,500 acres of land between the cities of Fillmore and Moorpark. The fire, which was started by a smoldering compost pile, was driven the first couple of days by a moderate Santa Ana wind event with northeast wind gusts to around 55 mph reported in and around the fire. The fire fortunately only destroyed one out building, but did result in 10 injuries. The total cost to suppress the fire was nearly $10 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the coast coupled with daytime heating brought afternoon thunderstorms with hail up to 3/4 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable air ahead of an upper-level low centered just off the coast of Northern California triggered bands of elevated convection which stretched from San Diego to the San Bernardino County deserts. Over 1500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes were recorded during the event, resulting in one death and multiple injuries. The lightning also started approximately 70 fires, most of which were small and did not result in any damage or injuries. The storms also produced gusty winds and hail, with one cell producing quarter-sized hail in the Del Mar area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 31 year old male suffered serious injuries while doing landscape work at a home on Camden Place in San Marcos when lightning struck a palm tree nearby.  He was transported to the hospital in critical condition. His 48 year old brother was treated for minor injuries.
